coredata.dc:title: Enhanced fluorescence of nano polyethylene glycol derived from the oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Raohao
Zhang, Changchang
Kuai, Yong
Liu, Xiang
Zhang, Changhua
coredata.dc:description: The newly emerging fluorescent materials attracted extensive interests such as carbon quantum dots or nano dots. When the fluorescent mechanism was discussed contribution of oxidation of starting materials for the fluorescence was neglected usually. Herein, fluorescent polyethylene glycol nano particles (PEG NPs) could be prepared when the solution was suffered from a convenient oven-heating at 100 °C for 60 min and the fluorescent quantum yield reached as high as 13.67% while PEG solution had none of fluorescence as is known to all. Measurements of infrared and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra illustrated the oxidation of PEG during the heating disposing. The fluorescent intensity could be improved successively at the presence of hydrogen peroxides or cobalt ions respectively. Such an intensive oxidation even resulted in generation of the carbon quantum dots. However, the added hydroquinone, as an antioxidant, prohibited the increase of fluorescence. Such evidences disclosed that oxidation was the key step for the fluorescence and for the formation of carbon quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylene glycol
Oxidation
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Hydroquinone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Oven-heating on PEG solution at varied temperatures
Reflux-heating on PEG solution at the absence of or in the presence of hydroquinone
Oven-heating on PEG solution in the presence of H2O2 or cobalt nitrate
Fluorescent quenching of Fe3+ and NO2 − on PEG NPs-2
Enhanced fluorescence when aqueous PEG is oven heated
Oxidation of PEG and the enhanced fluorescence
Fluorescent quenching of PEG NPs-2 solution in the presence of Fe3+ or NO2 –

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province ( KJ2017A047 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) corresponding to fluorescence spectra of the PEG solution under varied excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 380 nm and UV–vis spectra respectively before and after the oven-heating at 180 °C for 60 min; (c) Fluorescence spectra of the PEG solution under oven-heating at varied temperatures (excitation wavelength = 340 nm).

Integrated fluorescent intensity (excitation wavelength = 340 nm) of the samples prepared under different conditions versus the respective absorbance (< 0.05). (a–c) corresponding to oven-heating at 180, 140 and 100 °C respectively; and (d) corresponding to oven-heating at 100 °C in the presence of H2O2. The slopes of the regressed lines are also displayed in the figure.

IR spectra of the samples. (a) raw PEG; (b–e) corresponding to the PEG solution suffered from oven-heating for 60 min at 100 °C, 180 °C, mixed with H2O2 at 100 °C, mixed with cobalt nitrate at 100 °C respectively. Region of 1800–1600 cm−1 is magnified to have a clear view on the changes when the raw PEG is suffered from the lasting oven-heating.

XPS of C 1s of PEG and PEG NPs-1 corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively. The region from 288.0 to 290.5 eV of PEG and PEG NPs-1 marked with dash rectangles are enlarged to have a clear observation in (c).

Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength = 340 nm) of PEG solution endured reflux-heating at 100 °C. (a–b) corresponding to heating time of 6 and 4 h respectively; (c–d) corresponding to heating time of 6 and 4 h respectively in the presence of hydroquinone respectively.
TEM images of PEG NPs-1, PEG NPs-2 and PEG NPs-3 corresponding to (a), (b) and (c) respectively.
Fluorescence spectra of PEG NPs-1, PEG NPs-2 and PEG NPs-3 under varied excitation wavelength corresponding to (a–c) respectively (the respective concentration: 28.0, 25.0, 18.0 mg mL−1).

(a) Effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence spectra of PEG NPs-2 (excitation wavelength = 340 nm); (b) fluorescent quenching of Fe3+ on PEG NPs-2 solution; (c) regressed line between the fluorescent intensity and the concentration of Fe3+ of (b).

(a) Effect of different acid anions on the fluorescence spectra of PEG NPs-2 (excitation wavelength = 340 nm); (b) fluorescent quenching of NO2 – on PEG NPs-2 solution; (c) regressed line between the fluorescent intensity and the concentration of NO2 – of (b).

head.ce:title: Enhanced fluorescence of nano polyethylene glycol derived from the oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The newly emerging fluorescent materials attracted extensive interests such as carbon quantum dots or nano dots. When the fluorescent mechanism was discussed contribution of oxidation of starting materials for the fluorescence was neglected usually. Herein, fluorescent polyethylene glycol nano particles (PEG NPs) could be prepared when the solution was suffered from a convenient oven-heating at 100 °C for 60 min and the fluorescent quantum yield reached as high as 13.67% while PEG solution had none of fluorescence as is known to all. Measurements of infrared and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra illustrated the oxidation of PEG during the heating disposing. The fluorescent intensity could be improved successively at the presence of hydrogen peroxides or cobalt ions respectively. Such an intensive oxidation even resulted in generation of the carbon quantum dots. However, the added hydroquinone, as an antioxidant, prohibited the increase of fluorescence. Such evidences disclosed that oxidation was the key step for the fluorescence and for the formation of carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, newly emerging fluorescent materials such as carbon or graphene quantum dots and carbon nano dots caught extensive interests since the easy preparation, lower cost and fluorescent feature. Various small molecules could be integrated under hydrothermal conditions producing carbon quantum dots [1,2] or nano dots [3,4]. Sheets of graphene oxides could be split into nano pieces under harsh conditions [5,6] and combined chemically producing quantum dots, which possessed colorful fluorescence. Although many researches had reported their preparing techniques and the applications the explanation for fluorescent mechanism still faced some challenges. With respect to chemical structure, they distinguished with conventional fluorescent dyes terrifically. What was the real cause for the resulted fluorescence?

The intermolecular combination or crosslink at certain case was responsible for the resulted fluorescence, which put forward by Yang groups [7–9]. They defined this as crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE). Some macromolecules could be cross linked producing polymer nano particles via the reaction of functional groups such as hydroxyls [9]. The chains of polyacrylic acid could be cross linked together via the connection of ethanediamine [10]. Carbon tetrachloride could link the primary amino groups of polyvinylamine [11]. The crosslink herein should be defined as a complicate chemical combination. The produced polymer nano particles possessed an enhanced fluorescence compared with the corresponding precursors. Yang groups ascribed CEE to crosslink of the precursors and considered that the molecular rigidity decreased the energy loss when excited electrons transmit back to the ground states [7].

In fact, carbon quantum dots or nano dots were the derivation of complicate chemical combination of small molecules such as citric acid and glycine [12], urea and p-phenylenediamine [13], citric acid and diethylenetriamine [14], etc. Some of these raw materials had potential reactive groups. For example, carboxyl groups of one component could combine with amino groups of other component. Or the combination occurred among themselves such as citric acids [15]. Depending on these reactions, chemical combination proceeded smoothly. Of course, it needed intensive conditions in most cases. Such chemical combinations were helpful for the generation of molecular aggregation that enhanced the molecular rigidity. From this perspective, CEE theory might be an explanation for the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots or nano dots. However, some of the precursors did not possess mutual reactive groups such as urea and p-phenylenediamine [13], glucose and monopotassium phosphate [16], etc. How did the chemical combination realize?

It was possible that the precursors were oxidized under synthesized conditions at the initial stage of the chemical combination. The oxidation of precursors was inevitable due to the contact with dissolved oxygen or ambient oxygen [13,17]. Some amino-contained compounds were the typical examples since they were inclined to be oxidized by the environmental oxygen [13]. On account of this, such compounds were stored with isolation of air such as aniline. The oxidation enhanced the fluorescent intensity tremendously according to our researches on polyamide amine [18]. Additionally, the branched chains of polyamide amine favored the crosslink when the solution was suffered from a lasting heating. The oxidation and crosslink of polyamide amine contributed the enhancement of fluorescence.

Even stable substance in natural environment such as poly(ethylene oxide) tended to be oxidized in some cases. The alkyl chains of poly(ethylene oxide) (lower molecular weight of poly(ethylene oxide) called polyethylene glycol, PEG) could be oxidized by ambient oxygen when heated in an air circulating oven [19]. When poly(ethylene oxide) powder was suffered from thermal oxidation at 60 °C for tens of days the peak at ~1720 cm–1 changes obviously [20]. The ester adsorption at 1747 cm–1 arisen with the further oxidation. During the initial period of the degradation of poly(ethylene oxide) the oxidation caused the occurrence of hydroperoxide and the subsequent decomposition generating oxygenated groups along with concomitant chain scission. As a matter of fact, PEG could be a candidate material for preparing carbon nano dots [21].

Hence, oxidation, chemical combination and crosslink of the precursors might be the key responsibilities for the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots or nano dots. Herein, we tried to illustrate the oxidation of precursor were the essential step for the fluorescent enhancement of PEG nano particles.

PEG (Mn 300), hydrogen peroxide solution (30 wt% in H2O), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (99%), hydroquinone (99.5%), iron chloride hexahydrate (99%) and sodium nitrite (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China) and used without farther purification. Double distilled water was used throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oven-heating on PEG solution at varied temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 116 mg mL−1 aqueous solution of PEG was prepared. Then measured 1 mL of such solution mixed with 4 mL water. The mixture was put into a stainless plate, which was placed in an air circulating oven at 180 °C for 60 min. In the end, the water was evaporated completely. The remaining was dissolved with water and the fluorescence spectrum was estimated.

In order to investigate the effect of heating temperature on the fluorescent intensity of such disposed PEG, similar experiments were repeated at 140, 100 and 60 °C respectively for 60 min. The fluorescence spectra of these samples were measured respectively and had a comparison in fluorescent intensities. The product obtained at 100 °C was denoted with PEG NPs-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reflux-heating on PEG solution at the absence of or in the presence of hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the real cause of fluorescent occurrence of PEG described in 2.2 section, similar experiment was executed with a reflux-heating. The PEG solution (116 mg mL−1, 10 mL) was suffered from reflux-heating at 100 °C for a predetermined time. During the reflux-heating the water component could not be removed, which differed from the way of oven-heating. The variations of the fluorescence spectra of the PEG solution with the heating time were investigated.

We doubted the fluorescence of the disposed PEG had something to do with the oxidation of PEG, such an experiment with reflux-heating was repeated in the presence of 5.0 mg hydroquinone for inquire antioxidant of hydroquinone on the fluorescent intensity of the PEG solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oven-heating on PEG solution in the presence of H2O2 or cobalt nitrate
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of disclosing whether the oxidation of PEG was the real cause for the enhanced fluorescence, the PEG solution of 1 mL (116 mg mL−1) were mixed with 1 mL H2O2 solution (wt 30%) and 3 mL water. The total volume was 5 mL which was the same as the one described in 2.2 section. The experiment was executed with oven-heating at 100 °C for 60 min. The product was denoted with PEG NPs-2. We also repeated such an experiment in the presence of cobalt nitrate (3 mmol L−1 in the mixture) at 100 °C for 60 min to inquire the effect of Co2+ on fluorescent intensity since Co2+ could accelerate the oxidation of alkyl chain which utilized in crosslink of paint. The product was represented with PEG NPs-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching of Fe3+ and NO2 − on PEG NPs-2
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent stability of PEG NPs-2 (3 mL, 25.0 mg mL–1) was checked with adding 200 μL of K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+ or Fe3+ (400 μM for all) respectively. Similar experiment was repeated with respective adding 50 μL of PO4 3–, SO4 2–, NO3 –, HSO3 – or NO2 – (12 mM for all) to estimate the fluorescent stability of PEG NPs-2.

The fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) features of samples were measured on fluorescent and ultraviolet spectrometer (LS 45, PerkinElmer; Specord 200, Analytic Jena AG) respectively. Fluorescent quantum yields (QY) of the samples were calculated based on comparison with standard substance of quinine sulfate in 0.5 mol L−1 sulfuric acid (QY = 55.00%). The slopes of the regressed line between integrated fluorescent intensity and absorbance of the samples and standard substance were used in calculation of fluorescent QY, which described in detail in previous report [18]. The structural alteration of PEG when it suffered from lasting heating was revealed by infrared (IR) and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS). The morphology of PEG NPs-1, PEG NPs-2 and PEG NPs-3 were disclosed by images of high-resolution transmission microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced fluorescence when aqueous PEG is oven heated
ce:section.ce:para: When aqueous solution of PEG is heated at 180 °C for 60 min in an oven its fluorescent intensity enhances extremely while the fluorescence of the raw PEG solution without heating is hard to be detected, which is shown in Fig. 1(a). Obviously, the fluorescent intensities and the peaks vary with the excitation wavelengths. The maximum corresponds with the excitation of 320 nm. The raw PEG solution has a rather weak absorbance while the heated PEG solution performs an intensive absorption in region from 200 to 350 nm, which exhibited in Fig. 1(b). This result along with the fluorescent occurrence puzzles us since such a convenient oven-heating operation brings an unexpected increase of fluorescence. Fig. 1(c) tells us that the fluorescent intensity of the heated PEG solution alters with the heating temperature. When the temperature reaches 60 °C the fluorescent peak arise and the intensity increase successively with the enhanced temperature. The fluorescent peak of 180 °C is much more intensive than the one of 60 °C. These data tell us that the oven-heating that induces the intensive fluorescence of PEG solution. But what is the real reason for this phenomenon.

The relationship between integrated fluorescent intensity and absorbance of the heated PEG at 180, 140 and 100 °C respectively is measured and exhibited in Fig. 2. The fluorescent QY calculated according the slope of the regressed line (a–c) reach 6.87, 5.93% and 13.76% respectively. Although the increased temperature leads to the increase of fluorescent intensity the QY of the sample does not increase with the heating temperature. This is mainly due to the higher temperature arouses the more increase of the absorbance. Hence QY of the sample heated at 100 °C (PEG NPs-1) is the highest.

Fig. 1 demonstrates that the facile heating operation causes the terrific enhancement of fluorescence of PEG without any addition of chemicals. As is well known to all, PEG is stable enough in natural environment and has none of any fluorescence. The occurrence of fluorescence means the variation of PEG structure. Herein, since the PEG solution is heated in an oven with an air-blowing device we doubt oxygen involves in the chemical changes of PEG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation of PEG and the enhanced fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: When PEG solution is disposed with oven-heating at 180 °C for 60 min the main variation of IR spectra of the samples can be observed in region between 1800–1600 cm−1 shown in Fig. 3(c). It shows a clear peak at 1721 cm−1, which should be attributed to stretching vibration of C˭O (v(C˭O)) [4,6]. Also there is a shoulder peak at 1747 cm−1 [20]. The raw PEG does not possess these characteristic absorptions revealed by Fig. 3(a). The PEG endured lasting oven-heating at 100 °C of 60 min has the similar changes in v(C˭O) shown in Fig. 3(b) although the absorption of v(C˭O) is weaker than that of Fig. 3(c). However, the characteristic absorptions of Fig. 3(a–c) in finger regions are very similar. The IR data exhibit the negligible changes of PEG structure. Summarily, Fig. 3(a–c) disclose the involvement of oxygen when PEG solution endure the lasting heating. During the oven-heating, the water component evaporated gradually the dissolved and ambient oxygen may involve in the oxidation of PEG. The methylene in PEG chain nearing the oxygen atom may be prone to be oxidized more easily.

The oxidation of PEG is also witnessed by measurement of XPS shown in Fig. 4. The atomic percent of oxygen of PEG NPs-1 reaches 34.58% which is more than that of 33.79% of PEG. This suggests the involvement of ambient oxygen during the lasting heating at 100 °C. XPS of Fig. 4(a) shows the two peaks at 284.7 [14,22] and 286.2 eV [3] which derived from C 1s of alkyl and –CH2–O– respectively. Similarly, 284.9 and 286.4 eV in (b) should also belong to such components of PEG NPs-1. When the region from 288.0 to 290.5 eV of (a) and (b) is magnified the peak of 289.1 eV is observed clearly in (c). This peak should belong to −C˭O [3,23], which agrees with aforementioned analysis of IR spectra of Fig. 3(a–c). Hence both IR spectra and XPS measurements confirm the chemical changes of PEG when heated at 100 °C for 60 min.

When PEG solution is reflux-heated for 6 or 4 h the fluorescent intensity of the solution is also enhanced remarkably which exhibited in Fig. 5(a) and (b) respectively. Nevertheless, when PEG solution is mixed with 5.0 mg hydroquinone and disposed at the same conditions the enhanced fluorescent intensities depress more than a half, which shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d) respectively. Hydroquinone, as a common antioxidant, is capable to remove the produced hydroperoxide instantly and resist the self polymerization of some monomers. That prolongs the storage life of the monomers. In this case, hydroquinone may consume the hydroperoxide of PEG that restricts the oxidation of PEG. Fig. 5 reveals that hydroquinone restrain the fluorescent enhancement, which predicts the enhancement of fluorescence has a close relationship with the oxidation of PEG..

Combining the aforementioned analyses on IR spectra of Fig. 3(a–c), C 1 s spectra of Fig. 4 and fluorescence spectra of Fig. 5, it is apparent to justify that the oxidation of PEG devotes a great deal for the enhanced fluorescence. During the heating operation, hydroperoxides occur initially due to the oxidation of methylene. The terminal methylene (−OCH2 CH 2−OH) is oxidized giving −OCH2 C(OOH)H−OH firstly generating terminal carboxyl (−OCH2C(=O)−OH) accompanied by the dehydration. The carboxyl combines with terminal hydroxyl of another PEG via esterification. The weak peaks of 1747 and 1721 cm−1 of Fig. 3(b) should be due to the v(C=O) of ester and carboxyl respectively [20].

TEM image of Fig. 6(a) shows the generation of PEG nano particles when PEG solution is oven-heated at 100 °C for 60 min. The crosslink of PEG molecules should be arisen from the combination of the resulted terminal carboxyl and hydroxyl of PEG, as is analyzed above. Hence, the size of PEG NPs-1 reaches several hundred nanometers, shown in Fig. 6(a). When PEG solution is suffered from 100 °C for 60 min in the present of H2O2, the liberated oxygen of H2O2 may speed up the oxidation of PEG. Apparently, the size of PEG NPs-2 exhibited in Fig. 6(b) and Fig. S1 of Supporting Information is much less than that of PEG NPs-1. This suggests the scission of PEG chain with the help of strong oxidant of H2O2. The oxidation of the methylene of the repeat unit of −OCH2 CH 2−O− causes the degradation of PEG chains [24]. IR spectrum of Fig. 3(d) displays similar characteristic peaks at 1747 and 1721 cm−1. These also are due to the v(C=O) of ester and carboxyl respectively. The crystalline planes of PEG NPs-2 can also be distinguished clearly in Fig. 6(b) and Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. They should belong to typical carbon quantum dots [1,25]. And the fluorescent QY reaches 23.00% according to the slope of the regressed line in Fig. 2(d).

Additionally, stability of the fluorescence of PEG NPs-1 and PEG NPs-2 in acidic and alkaline media are checked respectively, which presented in Fig. S2 and S3 of Supporting Information. In aqueous media, both PEG NPs-1 and PEG NPs-2 solution are acidic (pH = 6.0 and 5.5 respectively). The faint acid should be derived from the resulted –COOH, as is consistent with aforementioned IR analyses. The varity of pH value causes slight changes of the fluorescent intensity of PEG NPs-1 and PEG NPs-2.

Experiments also demonstrate the oxidation of PEG can be accelerated by cobalt nitrate. As we all know that some heavy metal ions such as Co2+ can accelerate the oxidation and thus helps for solidifying of paint. IR spectrum of Fig. 3(e) exhibits that v(C˭O) of 1747 and 1721 cm−1 of PEG NPs-3. That also proves the oxidation in the presence of Co2+. Fig. 6(c) and Fig. S4 of Supporting Information show the size of PEG NPs-3 is much less than that of PEG NPs-1. This discloses chain scission of PEG which is accelerated by Co2+ as well.

Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of PEG NPs-3 shown in Fig. 7(c) is more intensive than that of both PEG NPs-1 and PEG NPs-2 exhibited in Fig. 7(a) and (b) respectively. With varied excitation wavelengths, the emission peaks of PEG NPs-2 almost remain unchanged. That suggests the comparative homogeneous size particle size of PEG NPs-2 which reflected by TEM image of Fig. 6(b).

Summarily, Fig. 3(b) and Fig. 4(b) reveal the structural alterations of PEG when it is heated in oven at a lower temperature of 100 °C. The newly occurred band v(C˭O) of IR and the C 1 s XPS predicts the oxidation of methylene of PEG. Even the reflux-heating on PEG solution brings about the occurrence of fluorescence. However, the fluorescence can be restricted by hydroquinone dramatically, which demonstrated by Fig. 5. Hence it is concluded that the oxidation of PEG is responsible for the enhanced fluorescence. Of course, the oxidation of PEG also leads to scission of PEG chain and successive intermolecular combination. Such complicate chemical combinations induce the intermolecular crosslink and the production of carbon quantum dots.

Based on the aforementioned analyses, the CEE theory [7,8,10,11] should include the contribution of oxidation of starting material for the intermolecular crosslink under the synthesized conditions and comprise the devotions of oxidation and crosslink for the enhanced fluorescence rather than only focusing on crosslink which improves molecular rigidness. Oxidation is inevitable during the intermolecular crosslink under the synthesized conditions such as a high temperature. Environmental or dissolved oxygen can involve in the reaction which is non-negligible for the enhanced fluorescence in the light of our experiments. In this work, the crosslink takes place for sure when PEG solution is oven-heated for 60 min. However, such a crosslink is derived from the oxidation of methylene of PEG. More intensive oxidation in the presence of H2O2 or Co2+ leads the break of PEG chains and the intermolecular crosslink of these broken species. The increase of molecular rigidity and the augment of oxygen-contained group contribute enhanced fluorescent intensity.

Therefore, when the fluorescent mechanism of carbon quantum dots or carbon nano dots is discussed the oxidation of raw material should not be ignored [26]. The starting materials may suffer from oxidation and successive chemical combination or oxidation, successive scission of molecular skeleton and final chemical combination. Such complicate chemical combinations cause intermolecular crosslink, which also raises the rigidity of quantum dots or nano dots. In fact, the oxidation enhances the fluorescence has been demonstrated by amino-contained compounds [13,18]. Therefore, the oxidation and skeleton rigidity may be the two principal causes for the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots or nano dots. The resulted oxygen-contained functional groups provide the reactivity for the chemical combination. And the p orbital of oxygen atom can be involved in conjugated system generating p-π conjugation that is also helpful for the fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching of PEG NPs-2 solution in the presence of Fe3+ or NO2 –
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent intensity of PEG NPs-2 solution are almost remained at the presence of several metal cations including K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Co2+. However, Fe3+ quenches the fluorescence of PEG NPs-2 obviously shown in Fig. 8(a). That illustrates fluorescence of PEG NPs-2 can be utilized in measuring Fe3+ in aqueous solution [27,28]. And with the increase of concentration of Fe3+ the fluorescent intensities decrease gradually exhibited in Fig. 8(b). Moreover, the relationship between fluorescent intensities and the concentration can be regressed linearly in range of 0–60 μM with expression of fluorescent intensity = –5.255 × concentration + 707.743 (square of the correlation coefficient = 0.95). It provides a convenient way of measuring Fe3+. That is useful in detect trace of Fe3+ in biological process.

Also, fluorescence of PEG NPs-2 solution is rather stable in the presence of several acid anions such as PO4 3–, SO4 2–, NO3 – and HSO3 –. Nevertheless, NO2 – decreases the intensity, which presented in Fig. 9(a). Additionally, the decrease of fluorescent intensity is linear with the increased concentration of NO2 –, which exhibited in Fig. 9(b) and (c). The regressed line in (c) reveals that intensity = –24.177 × concentration + 744.445 and square of the correlation coefficient = 0.99. It provides a facile way of detecting trace NO2 –.
These results claim that excited state of PEG NP-2 is sensitive to trace of Fe3+ or NO2 –. Energy loss occurs obviously when excited electrons transit back to the ground state.

PEG without any fluorescence performs a strong emission when suffered from a period of heating disposing. The principal cause for the fluorescence is the oxidation of PEG based on the analyses. At the initial stage, oxidation improves the density of oxygen-contained group that creates probabilities for the crosslink of PEG chain. More intensive oxidation leads to scission of PEG chains that is beneficial for successive chemical combination, crosslink of the species and generation of carbon quantum dots with enhanced fluorescence. Additionally, oxidation produces carbonyl groups that create regional conjugated parts. Presumably, oxidation may be a universal principle for the fluorescent of unconventional material such as carbon quantum dots or nano dots. Carbon quantum dots were usually prepared by hydrothermal, microwave or other violent technique which accelerates or intensifies the oxidation process. Hence, oxidations and crosslink may be the real reasons for the fluorescence of these unconventional substances. However, this theme is rarely discussed according to recent reports.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (KJ2017A047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.01.067.
